summary: I was born in Somalia, specifically in the northern part. I left when I was seven years old and didn’t return until February 2014. When I went back, I was surprised to see how stable and peaceful it had become, with tall buildings and clean streets, very different from the dangerous image I had in my mind due to the Somali Civil War. Somalia is recovering and has great hospitality, fresh food, and diversity that many people don’t expect. I want others to know that Somalia is not what the media often portrays, and they shouldn’t be afraid to visit.

When I was seven, my family and I moved to Nairobi, Kenya. We lived there until 2006 when my brother in the United States sponsored us to come to America. Nairobi was our home during my childhood after Somalia, and it was quite different from the U.S. I remember the contrast between life in Nairobi and what I experienced later in America, especially regarding rules and order.

Kenya was where my family settled after leaving Somalia, and we lived in Nairobi for several years. I noticed that life in Kenya was very different from America, especially in how people follow rules. For example, in Kenya, people often do what they want without strict adherence to lines or order, which was a big change when I came to the U.S. in 2006. Kenya was an important part of my journey before moving to America.

In 2006, my brother who was living in the United States sponsored my family to come here. We first arrived in Chicago and stayed in a hotel, which was very fancy compared to what I was used to. Then we moved to Minneapolis, Minnesota, where my brother had rented a house for us. America felt like the land of opportunities, and I was amazed by how organized and rule-oriented life was here. I graduated from Edison High School in 2010 and have been living in Minneapolis since.

Minneapolis became my new home after we arrived in the United States in 2006. My brother had rented a house here, and I was very happy to see him after a long time. I remember going to an all-you-can-eat buffet with him, which was a new experience for me. I started attending Edison High School here and graduated in 2010. Minneapolis is where I currently live and continue my studies.

Minnesota is the state where Minneapolis is located, and it became my family's new home after we moved from Kenya in 2006. My brother was already living here and helped us settle in. I attended Edison High School in Minnesota and graduated in 2010. The state has been important in my journey as an immigrant and student.

When we first arrived in the United States in 2006, we stayed one night in Chicago at a hotel. It was my first time seeing such a fancy place, very different from what I knew in Kenya. People helped us get to the hotel and the airport, and I was amazed by the warm welcome. Chicago was just a brief stop before we moved on to Minneapolis, but it left a strong impression on me.

I started attending Edison High School in Minneapolis in 2006 after moving to Minnesota. It was an important part of my life as I adapted to a new country and culture. I graduated from Edison High School in 2010, which was a proud moment for me and my family. The school helped me build a foundation for my future studies and career.

The Somali Civil War was a big reason why my family left Somalia when I was young. I had heard many frightening stories about Somalia being dangerous and unstable because of the war. However, when I returned in 2014, I saw a very different picture—Somalia was recovering and peaceful. The war shaped much of my early life and the perceptions I had about my homeland.
